Je ne suis pas certain du besoin d’un meta-repo s’il y a des tags. En revanche, je ne suis pas non plus certain de l’intérêt du tag par rapport au meta-repo.

Si on prend du recul, le seul besoin est d’avoir une référence unique à un état spécifique de tous les modules qui composent OpenFisca, dans la mesure où ils sont interdépendants. La solution la plus courante dans ce genre de cas est la publication sur un registre public d’un paquet référençant un état de chacun des modules.

À défaut, s’il y a une stratégie de versionnement qui assure que tous les modules partagent un numéro de version unique (x.y.z d’un module est compatible avec x.y.z de tous les autres), je ne vois pas l’intérêt d’un meta-repo : chaque dépôt contient la référence qui est nécessaire pour déterminer la version des autres avec laquelle il est censé interagir. Dans ce cas, le coût administratif me semble démesuré : une modification sur un module implique un tagging sur tous les dépôts. Ce serait mitigeable avec `gitslave`, par exemple, mais au vu du nombre de modifs sur `openfisca-france`, on va à mon avis rapidement surtagger tous les autres dépôts.

Si le numéro de version n’est pas partagé, il faut effectivement un espace dans lequel inscrire quel numéro de version d’un module correspond à quels numéros de version des autres modules. Mais dans ce cas, quel est l’intérêt du tag ? S’il n’est pas sémantique, utiliser un SHA ou un tag est équivalent, et on évite alors le coût administratif du tag.

class ShortInitialSniffTimeoutTransport(Transport):

What is it that you are trying to achieve here? The code you are using is running a `bulk` operation via `streaming_bulk` and then trying to index the results of the operation by using another `bulk` operation. I assume that is not what you want.

If you just want to index your documents just choose one - either `streaming_bulk` or `bulk`. They act a little differently - `streaming_bulk` returns an iterator that will iterate over the results for individual documents whereas `bulk` will just return the number of documents that succeeded and failed.

    87dc:	f010 0f01 	tst.w	r0, #1
    87e0:	bf1a      	itte	ne

```
`mrs` and `svc` are not supported. I got this during symbolic execution:
```
defaultdict(<type 'list'>, {'active': [<SimState @ 0x87d9>], 'unsat': [], 'deadended': [], 'pruned': [], 'unconstrained': []})
<State errored with "Empty IRSB passed to SimIRSB.">
0x87d9:	mrs	r0, control

0x87e5:	svc	#2

0x87e7:	movs	r0, #1
```
In fact, I added hook methods for those instructions: I print the error at the entry of the hook function, emulate the behavior of the instruction, then increment `ip` to resume normal execution. As you can see, the execution continues at the next instruction. However, it failed on `movs` at 0x87e7, while this is supposed to be supported. I guess it's because of the jumpkind: it was `Ijk_NoDecode` when VEX tries to lift `svc` instruction, and remains the same after the hook function, since I didn't change it. Indeed, moving the `Ijk_NoDecode` to `SimIRSBError` solve this.

Hey @manu-chroma I think details below can help anyone who wants to set up a virtual environment for the development purposes without messing up the original installations done on their systems: 
## Notes from Code fest:
To work for development purposes, you can install cwltool in a virtual environment: 
* Install virtualenv via pip: `pip install virtualenv`
* Clone the cwltool: `git clone https://github.com/common-workflow-language/cwltool.git`
* Switch to cwltool directory: `cd cwltool`
* Create a virtual environment: `virtualenv cwltool`
* To begin using the virtual environment, it needs to be activated: `source bin/activate`
* To check if you have the virtual environment set up: `type type` and `type python`
* Install cwltool in the virtual environment: `pip install .`
* Check the version which might be different from the version installed in general on any system: `cwltool --version`

> sky region -> pixel region -> mpl shape -> draw

This would be my preference. See example lower down.

> There's maybe an issue with how to expose an option that controls the number of points, or how to polygonise shapes well?

The plan was that this was a kwarg in the ``to_pixel`` method for sky regions (see ``tolerance`` [here](https://github.com/astropy/regions/blob/master/regions/core/core.py#L270))

So just to clarify, this is how it could work:

```python
>>> reg = SkyCircleRegion(...)
>>> preg = reg.to_pixel(mode='local')  # returns a PixelCircleRegion
>>> ax.add_patch(preg)  # adds a Circle patch in pixel space
```

```python
>>> reg = SkyCircleRegion(...)
>>> preg = reg.to_pixel(mode='affine')  # returns an PixelEllipseRegion
>>> ax.add_patch(preg)  # adds an Ellipse patch in pixel space
```

```python
>>> reg = SkyCircleRegion(...)
>>> preg = reg.to_pixel(mode='full')  # returns an PixelPolygonRegion
>>> ax.add_patch(preg)  # adds a Polygon patch in pixel space
```

In the latter example, ``to_pixel`` can optionally take a tolerance.

One could optionally then just define ``as_patch`` for sky regions to be:

```python
def as_patch(self, mode='local', tolerance=0, **kwargs):
    pixel_reg = self.to_pixel(mode=mode, tolerance=tolerance)
    return pixel_reg.as_patch(**kwargs)
```

which would simplify the code above while making sure the sky -> pixel conversion is only defined once, and we never have to rely on fancy WCSAxes classes.

I do not completely agree with this article. I actually use PBR to abstract this setup.py-ish complexity and works great for both libraries and applications (it is widely used on many OpenStack projects). Hardcoding stuff in setup.py should be considered as bad practices. It is hard to parse, external services (such as readthedocs,...) cannot easily access to this information,... So if setup.py can be as simple as possible for maintainability sake, i'll be happy.

requirements.txt is a great improvement over hardcoded setup.py (even find_packages is hard). It allowed external tools to work with the package, and so much confort in edition.

My point is: **Libraries** ship with `requirements.txt` describing **compatibility versions by range**, **applications** ship with `requirements.txt` **freezing all versions**. I used a lot pip-tools to maintain `requirements.txt.in`, and Pipfile/pipenv makes life much easier now: libraries does not track `Pipfile.lock`, applications does. Work done. But for the moment all application does not support `Pipfile*` (first of all: PBR itself), so I keep requirements.txt synchronized (with version not frozen for libraries, and with version frozen for applications).
So for the dependencies part, the job is pretty much done without having to do kung-foo in `setup.py`.

Pipfile/pipenv is the future of python, and that is greatly expected!

Just for the interpreter itself, sadly, there are limitation: there is no way in `Pipfile` to tell "this pipfile works for python 3.5 and 3.6", for example. But this file can say "it works for Python 3.5". 

**TL;DR: Just a "or". We only would like to have a simple "or" syntax for python interpreter**
It is a small improvement that would help a lot.

Great find! I think this is a bug in the [gradient definition for `np.prod`](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/22285c1553c59eb1ed8647e674399765f10540f8/autograd/numpy/numpy_grads.py#L262-L265).

Specifically, I think [this line](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/22285c1553c59eb1ed8647e674399765f10540f8/autograd/numpy/numpy_grads.py#L264) assumes that `x != 0.` anywhere. That is, we're taking the gradient of something like `ans = x*y`, so just like in [`the gradient of __mul__`](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/22285c1553c59eb1ed8647e674399765f10540f8/autograd/core.py#L321) the gradient we return should be something like `g * y`. However, this code is effectively reconstructing `y` via `y = ans / x`, which is only valid where `x != 0`.

Yes, that's right. There's a graph to draw here, but I don't have your ascii art skills. For chain-structured functions, have three chains involved here: the top one is from the function eval, the middle one from the first call to `tf.gradients`, and the bottom one from the second call to `tf.gradients`. As you say, the bottom one doesn't actually depend on the values from the middle one, by linearity. Both the bottom chain and the middle chain depend on the values of the top chain.

Even better, there won't even be extra memory overhead at runtime: because the top chain and bottom chain have arrows pointing in the same direction (i.e. to the right), and the arrows from the top chain to the bottom don't skip steps, the TF graph executor is smart enough to reuse memory as it performs the evaluation, rather than evaluating things in a bad order and keeping too large an active set.

One way to convince yourself that `g` isn't evaluated at runtime is the fact that we don't even need to supply a value for `v` in `feed_dict`; that variable stays unbound, and we don't get an error because we never try to evaluate a node that depends on it.

Thanks for bringing this up, it was a choice I haven't really documented before. We actually started off with a set of CFFI bindings for librdkafka (the basis of which you can still find at https://bitbucket.org/yungchin/python-librdkafka/src - but I should add a note there that it's abandoned code). The problem with those was that in practice, it turned out that some calls into libffi were so expensive, that the pure pykafka code ran faster than the rdkafka-backed code.

In the profiling I did, I found that while the actual `consume()` calls were very fast, getting the payload out of the resulting `cdata` via a `cast()` (see [here][1] and [here][2]) turned out prohibitively slow. Note this was for CFFI on CPython - at the time we hadn't done any work towards pypy support yet - but I suspect the problem isn't any different on PyPy: the bottleneck seemed to be the handling of pointer dereferencing via libffi calls, which should work the same with both interpreters.

> Alternatively we can provide a pure python version and disallow building the extension under PyPy.

At build-time, we can't see if the intention is to use pypy at run-time. But certainly, using the C extension is currently not safe with pypy (I've noted this in the readme) and I'd recommend against it. The producer e.g. assumes that PyObject references are stable void pointers, which is not guaranteed on pypy.

Thanks

[1]: https://bitbucket.org/yungchin/python-librdkafka/src/8cf3c91bebfee4b3640735215ba85f22b08c73ec/rd_kafka/message.py?at=master&fileviewer=file-view-default#message.py-21
[2]: https://bitbucket.org/yungchin/python-librdkafka/src/8cf3c91bebfee4b3640735215ba85f22b08c73ec/rd_kafka/utils.py?at=master&fileviewer=file-view-default#utils.py-20

No, but only because the distinction between those two cases is *implicit* in the distinction between encoder and decoder.

The way this works is, a peer decides the maximum amount of resources it is willing to commit to *decoding* HPACK, and turns this into a value of SETTINGS_MAX_HEADER_TABLE_SIZE. It sends this setting to the other peer. The other peer receives that setting, and then selects how many resources it is willing to commit to *encoding* HPACK. It then sets that value in its HPACK encoder, which should then emit a table size changed field in the next header block it sends. The HPACK decoder should receive that value and change its header size accordingly.

This means that the HTTP/2 implementation should only ever explicitly set the size of its *encoder*, not its decoder. Its decoder should have its size informed by SETTINGS_MAX_HEADER_TABLE_SIZE. The HTTP/2 implementation *should*, however, ensure that the header table size on the decoder never exceeds SETTINGS_MAX_HEADER_TABLE_SIZE.

I've got a few thoughts on this, as I've been facing this issue recently.

As Cory notes, there's concern a user could lock in to a specific tag and never get updates. I don't think that's entirely bad thing, though, if the tags follow a good practice.

Here's what I'm thinking.

There are `latest` tag which represents the most current stable release.
There are tags for each specific versioned release, i.e., v1.0, v1.0.1, v1.0.2, v2.0, v2.1, v2.1.1
There is a release tag for each major version, i.e., release-v1, release-v2, release-v3

As a user, I expect stability in my interfaces. If I subscribe to `layer:mylayer@release-v1` I get whatever the last version of v1 is. Automatic updates for minor releases.

If I subscribe to `layer:mylayer` or `layer:mylayer@latest`, it pulls the latest table release, regardless of major version. Automatic updates for all releases.

I solved it.
tflearn is using an older api of tensorflow .
the older api ` tf.split(axis, num_or_size_splits, value)`
now is `tf.split(value, num_or_size_splits, axis)`
the param `value`and`axis`  **location is exchanged**
so you should go to `C:\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\tflearn\layers\recurrent.py, line 601`
find the function 
```
array_ops.split(1,2, **_linear([inputs, state],
                    2 * self._num_units, True, 1.0, self.weights_init,
                    self.trainable, self.restore, self.reuse))
```
and **change the location** of `value` and `axis` ,like this:
```
 array_ops.split( _linear([inputs, state],
                    2 * self._num_units, True, 1.0, self.weights_init,
                    self.trainable, self.restore, self.reuse),2,1)
```

Hi @cjydayang. First, it looks like you're missing a second dash in front of the "with" in "--with-selenium", but when using pytest instead of nosetests, you might not need that part at all. Second, if you're using "--with-testing_base", then by default all logs including screenshots will be stored UNLESS you individually pick out which logs you want. In your script above, you only put "--with-basic_test_info", which means SeleniumBase will only use that unless you also add "--with-screenshots". So either add that, or remove "--with-basic_test_info" (So that only "--with-testing_base" is used). Third, try using "--log_path=Log/" instead of "--log_path=./Log/" (or stick with the default "latest_logs"). Forth, the archived logs folder will stay empty unless you have ARCHIVE_EXISTING_LOGS set to True in https://github.com/seleniumbase/SeleniumBase/blob/master/seleniumbase/config/settings.py . That prevents the archived logs folder from getting too huge with screenshots. Here's a full command you can try to see if a "latest_logs" folder gets created with data: (from examples/ folder) "pytest test_fail.py --with-selenium --with-testing_base --browser=firefox". If that works fine, you can add in the "--log_path=Log/".

```jsx

I'll explore a couple of these for a sec

> User clicks the display area on <CodeCell id="2"/>, the state is now {editorFocusedCell: 1, cellFocused: 2}.

I think we want `editorFocusedCell` to be `null` in this case. We want focus from the cursor/editor to go away (prevent accidental typing, allow other text input to be used).

> User clicks the cell editor on <CodeCell id="2"/>, the state is now {editorFocusedCell: 2, cellFocused: 2}.

Here's where it's a bit strange (and where you fixed it in #1055). We want to lose focus on any other editors (maybe it doesn't have to be explicit) and allow CodeMirror to do the focusing so the user ends up clicking exactly at the cursor position they want.

# Recap There are many proposals to approach this issue. Since the target is to stack **any** icon on **any** icon, some approaches will not work and others will have drawbacks. No silver bullets here at the moment. ## Approach 1: Make new icons Pros: 1. Transparency 2. Pixel perfection 3. Simple markup (``) Cons: 1. Requires n² icons. **This approach is simply not doable.** --- ## Approach 2: Stack with text shadow #5027 Fiddle available: http://jsfiddle.net/tagliala/wvquLyo9/ Pros: 1. Transparency Cons: 1. No pixel perfection at 14px 2. No support for IE8 **and** IE9 http://caniuse.com/#feat=css-textshadow 3. Complex markup (stack) --- ## Approach 3: Stack with badges Repo available: http://alaind-.github.io/font-awesome-badge/ Pros: 1. Pixel perfection Cons: 1. No transparency 2. Poor support of IE8 3. Can't stack any icon on any icon 4. Very complex markup (stack with more than 2 icons) **This approach will not solve this issue** --- ## Approach 4: Svg cut Fiddle available: http://jsfiddle.net/tagliala/d42m5/ Pros: 1. Transparency 2. Will actually stack any icon on any icon with cutout Cons: 1. No pixel perfection 2. Poor support of browsers 3. Ugly rendering 4. Very very complex markup

[default_1]

Hello @delmottea 
For the R200 you can toggle `r200_emitter_enabled` option on and off, for SR300 you need `f200_laser_power`:
```cpp
dev.set_option(rs::option::r200_emitter_enabled, 0); //disable pattern
for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) dev.wait_for_frames(); // wait several frames for the effect to take place
// get frame data
dev.set_option(rs::option::r200_emitter_enabled, 0); //re-enable pattern
```
Please note that there is certain latency between when you set option and when you get the first frame reflecting that change.

In your release notes you still have this issues (#774) as an issue and then above you reference the intel communities where the last response from Intel is in January.  To my knowledge, This is the correct answer:

For HW Color/Depth Sync

1.  This will never be possible with the D435 since the RGB and Depth Sensor are on different PCB
2.   This is possible with D415 since the RGB and Depth sensors are on same PCB with the following:
     a. FW 5.9.1 or later supports HW sync on D415 
     b. RGB manual exposure must be used
     c.  There is a known bug that prevents the RGB gain from being adjust to achieve acceptable quality video
    d.  Until the gain is fixed (so that it can be set to levels that auto-exposure is achieved), HW sync for Color/Depth on D415 is not usable.

Hi @grlee77 

> why pass in details when it is not used?

I was locally testing a different threshold per subband and I forgot to remove the parameter before submitting the PR. Indeed, it doesn't make any difference here.

> There is no need to call `_universal_thresh` each time as the threshold is the same for all coefficient levels. I would store the threshold in a variable and then build the dict using that single threshold

> I think `method` should most likely be added as the last argument here to avoid breaking existing code.

> Please also add the following reference (section 4.2 is on VisuShrink)

Thanks for pointing this out. I will fix that.

> There may be some instances in which the VisuShrink result is preferred, but the BayesShrink case is almost always going to give a result with higher MSE, PSNR and mSSIM metrics.

While I can't think of any case where VisuShrink will give better results considering those metrics, it is a well known method commonly found in the literature and as a reference for comparing against other methods.

I read your suggestion on #1833  about implementing different thresholding methods. As you just said, while BayesShrink works fairly well on the general case, there may be special cases where others will be more suited. In page 2 of the article [1] referenced in the merged implementation it is said that BayesShrink a few times performed worse (though by only a 1% decrease of the MSE) than SUREShrink. NormalShrink [2] gives (slightly) better results than BayesShrink in several cases, and may offer a fair competition. It seems reasonable to offer some choices, especially when they are relatively easy to implement as only a small `_thresh` function and a condition block have to be added.

There are many other thresholding methods and adaptations of existing ones. If it's in the interest of skimage to extend the wavelet denoise functionality there should be a discussion of which to implement.

[1] Chang, S. Grace, Bin Yu, and Martin Vetterli. "Adaptive wavelet thresholding for image denoising and compression." IEEE Transactions on 9.9 (2000): 1532-1546. DOI: 10.1109/83.862633

[2] Kaur, L., Gupta, S., Chauhan, R. C., "Image Denoising using Wavelet Thresholding", Indian Conference on Computer Vision (2002), Graphics and Image Processing, Ahmedabad

@kne42 Since Python 3.5+ only supports type *annotations* as opposed to optional static typing, I proposed a similar concept for scikit-learn purely for readability. 

I'd be happy to help scikit-image make of these changes at on low-level algorithms. Since most of these algorithms accept multiple input types, I proposed something like this:

```python
import numpy as np
from typing import List, NewType

# Accepts either a one-dimensional array or a list of floats ...
Array1D = NewType('Array1D', np.ndarray, List[np.float64])

# Accepts either a two-dimensional array or a list of floats ...
Array2D = NewType('Array2D', np.ndarray, List[np.float64, np.float64])

# Define the function with appropriate type hints ...
def convolve_image(some_input: Array2D, param_one: int, param_two:float) -> Array2D:
    # some logic ...
```

This would require no extra dependencies and can be committed experimentally without consequence.

So that's how the bug manifests. But that's not all. If you look inside  `eth_endpoint_to_hostport()` you will see that the problem is actually in another function called [split_endpoint()](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/7338b8c320d40fc4deda11feb95403d7f2abb4cb/raiden/utils/__init__.py#L124).

The regular expression used does not take into account URLs like infura's that have a path after the endpoint.

```
>>> split_endpoint('mainnet.infura.io/accescode:8545')                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
('mainnet.infura.io', None)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
>>> endpoint = 'mainnet.infura.io/accescode:8545'                                                                                                                                                                                                                               
>>> import re                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   
>>> match = re.match(r'(?:[a-z0-9]*:?//)?([^:/]+)(?::(\d+))?', endpoint, re.I)                                                                                                                                                                                                  
>>> match.groups()                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
('mainnet.infura.io', None)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
>>> match = re.match(r'(?:[a-z0-9]*:?//)?([^:/]+)(?::(\d+))?', endpoint, re.I)                                                                                                                                                                                                  
>>> split_endpoint('mainnet.infura.io:8545/accescode')                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
('mainnet.infura.io', 8545)        
```

And that function is also used in some places in the code still:

```
raiden/network/discovery.py:108:21:        host_port = split_endpoint(endpoint)
raiden/ui/cli.py:571:34:    (listen_host, listen_port) = split_endpoint(listen_address)

raiden/ui/cli.py:1162:32:        (api_host, api_port) = split_endpoint(args['api_address'])
raiden/utils/__init__.py:123:5:def split_endpoint(endpoint: str) -> Tuple[str, Union[str, int]]:
raiden/utils/__init__.py:146:16:        return split_endpoint(rpc_host)
raiden/api/rest.py:306:32:                    _, _port = split_endpoint(web3)
raiden/api/rest.py:307:32:                    _host, _ = split_endpoint(host)
tools/scenario_runner.py:87:34:    (listen_host, listen_port) = split_endpoint(listen_address)
```

The comma is not extra. 
```python
for friend, in session.run("MATCH (a:Person {name:'Alice'})-[:KNOWS]->(x) RETURN x"):
    print('Alice says, "hello, %s"' % x["name"])
```
is equivalent to:
```python
for record in session.run("MATCH (a:Person {name:'Alice'})-[:KNOWS]->(x) RETURN a, x"):
    friend, = record
    print('Alice says, "hello, %s"' % friend["name"])
```
`Record.__iter__` will be called in order to assign the first returned value (`x`) to `friend`.

It's use is to extract returned values like so:
```python
for a, x in session.run("MATCH (a:Person {name:'Alice'})-[:KNOWS]->(x) RETURN a,x"):
    print('%s says, "hello, %s"' % (a["name"], x["name"]))
```
instead of having to address them explicitly in the record:
```python
for record in session.run("MATCH (a:Person {name:'Alice'})-[:KNOWS]->(x) RETURN a,x"):
    print('%s says, "hello, %s"' % (record["a"]["name"], record["x"]["name"]))
```

The problem is that `Record.__iter__` returns `iter(self._keys)` instead of `iter(self._values)`.

I believe this is a flaw in the implementation of the `Record` class.
https://github.com/neo4j/neo4j-python-driver/blob/f7c2f771c4b55ef6b81b38035e091c406a8aba46/neo4j/v1/session.py#L477

I may be wrong, I just started to evaluate this library, but I see no point in providing iteration support to fetch the already known variable names instead of the actual values.

It's *not really* `tmux` that is resetting `PATH`/`PROMPT_COMMAND`. The thing is that `tmux` runs login shells on default and thus `/etc/profile`, `/etc/profile.d/*`, ... get sourced, which then set those variables but (usually) won't touch other ones like `CONDA_*`.
So, basically compare the following outputs:
```bash
[maba@mb830 ~]$ PS1='>>>BASE SHELL $ '
>>>BASE SHELL $ echo $PATH
/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/usr/lib/jvm/default/bin:/usr/bin/site_perl:/usr/bin/vendor_perl:/usr/bin/core_perl
>>>BASE SHELL $ export PATH=/tmp/bin:$PATH
>>>BASE SHELL $ echo $PATH
/tmp/bin:/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/usr/lib/jvm/default/bin:/usr/bin/site_perl:/usr/bin/vendor_perl:/usr/bin/core_perl
>>>BASE SHELL $ bash
[maba@mb830 ~]$ echo $PATH
/tmp/bin:/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/usr/lib/jvm/default/bin:/usr/bin/site_perl:/usr/bin/vendor_perl:/usr/bin/core_perl
[maba@mb830 ~]$ exit
exit
>>>BASE SHELL $ bash --login
[maba@mb830 ~]$ echo $PATH
/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/usr/lib/jvm/default/bin:/usr/bin/site_perl:/usr/bin/vendor_perl:/usr/bin/core_perl
[maba@mb830 ~]$ exit
logout
>>>BASE SHELL $ echo $PATH
/tmp/bin:/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/usr/lib/jvm/default/bin:/usr/bin/site_perl:/usr/bin/vendor_perl:/usr/bin/core_perl
```
You can of course make `tmux` run another command instead of launching a login shell with things like `tmux new shell-command`, e.g., `tmux new bash`, which would then preserve `PATH`.

In my case, these instructions were not enough to get all packages working, because the accelerate package was forcing numexpr to be kept at oldie 2.4.4, instead of upgrading to 2.5 (and also pandas was linked to some wrong libs). 

While this is fixed, the following worked for me:

```
conda remove mkl-rt accelerate
conda install -f mkl
# note, this reinstalls accelerate but does not downgrade numexpr or reinstall mkl-rt.
# however, do not run conda update --all or conda update anaconda
conda install numpy scipy scikit-learn numexpr pandas numbapro mkl-service
conda update numbapro  
```

Even if I'm an academic, I also think that making mkl free is really sweet. Thanks!

`C:\User\UserName\AppData\local\Continuum\Anaconda2\` is +/-52 characters, leaving +/-208 user-usable characters. (The +/- part is 'actual username length' minus 9.)  The `C:\User\UserName\` portion seems like a reasonable requirement for single-user installs.  `AppData\local\Continuum\Anaconda2` is I'm sure Windows convention, and widely considered the "correct place" to install to.  [`appdirs`](https://github.com/ActiveState/appdirs) is my typical reference here, and it actually looks like we're not following the standard.  Looking now, for Windows 7, I see

```
>>> appname = "SuperApp"
>>> appauthor = "Acme"
>>> user_data_dir(appname, appauthor)
'C:\\Users\\trentm\\AppData\\Local\\Acme\\SuperApp'
```

Since local windows paths are case-insensitive, I guess this is consistent.

However... I'm not unsympathetic to the issue you raise @treetoc.  As a calibration point, I just looked at the longest file path for the django package, which can nest files fairly deep.

```
lib/python3.4/site-packages/django/contrib/auth/migrations/__pycache__/0007_alter_validators_add_error_messages.cpython-34.pyc
```

It's 126 characters.  Granted this is on unix, but at least it's a ballpark number.  In this case that leaves 134 characters left over for the `CONDA_PREFIX` it's being installed into.  The conventional location for named environments on Windows would be `C:\User\UserName\AppData\local\Continuum\Anaconda2\envs\`, 57 characters, so that leaves 77 characters remaining for the env name in this case.  That length seems reasonable to me.

If there are other specific examples you have, let's discuss them.  Given java's language conventions, I can envision a possible problem there.  We haven't really thought through what conda packages for java means, and the implications.  I haven't anyway.  Especially what type of prefix replacement is required to make the package portable, or if prefix replacement is even relevant.

The explanation for this behavior is that there is
1. different assignment of `features` in `conda-forge` compared to `defaults`, and
2. feature prioritization is handled differently for explicitly requested and dependency packages.

Regarding the first point, I assume @mcg1969 would say that, e.g., `fn: 'libpng-1.6.34-vc14_0', features: 'vc14', depends: ['vc 14', 'zlib 1.2.11']` on the `conda-forge` package is a wrong feature/dependency assignment...
The second point is just status quo and sadly leads to inconsistent behavior between installing a package explicitly or only as a dependency. (As an example for this, you can compare the outputs of `conda install -c conda-forge numpy` and `conda install -c conda-forge xnumpy`.)

----
The following are just the explanations I assume for the observed behavior:

----
Package information (URL, features, dependencies)
```bash
$ alias parse_info='python -c '"'"'import sys,json; print(*(" ".join(map(repr, (x["url"], x.get("features"), x["depends"]))) for x in sum(json.load(sys.stdin).values(), [])), sep="\n")'"'"
$ conda info --json gflags=2.2.1=h6538335_0 gflags=2.2.1=vc14_0 libpng=1.6.30=vc14_1 libpng=1.6.34=h79bbb47_0 libpng=1.6.34=vc14_0 zlib=1.2.8=vc14_3 zlib=1.2.11=h8395fce_2 vc=14=h0510ff6_3 | parse_info
'https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/main/win-64/gflags-2.2.1-h6538335_0.tar.bz2' None ['vc 14.*']
'https://conda.anaconda.org/conda-forge/win-64/gflags-2.2.1-vc14_0.tar.bz2' 'vc14' []
'https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/win-64/libpng-1.6.30-vc14_1.tar.bz2' None ['vc 14', 'zlib 1.2.*']
'https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/main/win-64/libpng-1.6.34-h79bbb47_0.tar.bz2' None ['vc 14.*', 'zlib >=1.2.11,<1.3.0a0']
'https://conda.anaconda.org/conda-forge/win-64/libpng-1.6.34-vc14_0.tar.bz2' 'vc14' ['vc 14', 'zlib 1.2.11']
'https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/main/win-64/vc-14-h0510ff6_3.tar.bz2' None ['vs2015_runtime >=14.0.25123,<15.0a0']
'https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/main/win-64/zlib-1.2.11-h8395fce_2.tar.bz2' None ['vc 14.*']
'https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/win-64/zlib-1.2.8-vc14_3.tar.bz2' None ['vc 14']
'https://conda.anaconda.org/conda-forge/win-64/zlib-1.2.8-vc14_3.tar.bz2' 'vc14' []
```

----
* maximize `(version, channel)` of requested packages (`zlib`):
  picks `zlib` from `defaults`
* maximize packages with features:
  picks `libpng`, `gflags` from `conda-forge` as those use the `vc14` feature
* maximize `(version, channel)` of dependencies
```
(shuffle) λ conda update zlib
...
    gflags: 2.2.1-h6538335_0 --> 2.2.1-vc14_0      conda-forge [vc14]  <-- !!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!
    libpng: 1.6.30-vc14_1    --> 1.6.34-vc14_0     conda-forge [vc14]  <-- !!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!
    zlib:   1.2.8-vc14_3     --> 1.2.11-h8395fce_2
```

----
* maximize `(version, channel)` of requested packages (`zlib`, `libpng`, `gflags`, ...):
  picks up all packages from `defaults` since featured packages are not maximized beforehand 

Versions tied for packages from both channels, hence the ones from higher priority channel are used. (This has to be done to avoid comparing build numbers across channels.)
```
(shuffle) λ conda update --all
...
    gflags: 2.2.1-vc14_0  conda-forge [vc14] --> 2.2.1-h6538335_0
    libpng: 1.6.34-vc14_0 conda-forge [vc14] --> 1.6.34-h79bbb47_0
```

Sorry I've been tardy on this PR.  @msarahan just got my attention, so thanks.  After a first review, I think it's good you're following the fish model.  I think that's generally the right direction for activate and deactivate to be going.  I do plan for `conda` to become a shell command everywhere in front of the normal bin/conda executable, hopefully in the near term future.

The biggest pain point I see right now is the root-prefix-level `xontribs` directory.  We generally try to follow FHS, and that side-steps the FHS scheme.  I'm especially thinking here the case of enterprise sysadmins, and when we move most all of conda to a private environment.  In that case, at least currently, the only files created in the root prefix and conda's initial install time will be an empty `conda-meta/history` file, symlinks for `bin/activate`, `bin/conda`, and `bin/deactivate` to `envs/_conda_/bin` and I guess `etc/fish.d/conf.d/conda.fish`.  That last file is one I think enterprise sysadmins will understand and be fine with.  It'll probably map eventually to similar `etc/profile.d/conda.sh` file for bourne shells and `etc/profile.d/conda.csh` for c shells.  I guess the `etc/` directory is the important part for configuration here, following FHS.

Are there any workable alternatives to that root-level `xontribs` directory for conda installs?

I have run into this same issue today as well. Removing the channel from the prefix is not enough to fix the error for me. I also have to add the following lines to the yaml file:

```
channels:
- channel_name
```

This can be seen in the [`from_yaml()`](https://github.com/conda/conda-env/blob/33c4e74f5cf6d4998e9aca1ddf26b3ce11f1df95/conda_env/env.py#L65) function.

I don't know enough about the conda source to make any edits myself, but following the source code (starting from the conda-env [`from_file()`](https://github.com/conda/conda-env/blob/33c4e74f5cf6d4998e9aca1ddf26b3ce11f1df95/conda_env/env.py#L68)), the line from the yaml is sent to [`conda.cli.common.spec_from_line()`](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/cli/common.py#L509), where it is parsed by the [regexp](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/cli/common.py#L495) above. It parses the name as "channel::package" which is obviously incorrect (same reason why `conda install channel::package` fails with the same error). I'm not sure if the name should be parsed properly here, or parsed properly further down the chain nearer to where it is actually loaded.

The easiest fix from my perspective would be to loop through all of the parsed dependencies in the [`Environment` constructor](https://github.com/conda/conda-env/blob/33c4e74f5cf6d4998e9aca1ddf26b3ce11f1df95/conda_env/env.py#L132), find which ones have the `::`, and remove the channel from the package name string. The channel could then be added to the Environment using the [add_channels()](https://github.com/conda/conda-env/blob/33c4e74f5cf6d4998e9aca1ddf26b3ce11f1df95/conda_env/env.py#L138) method.

The problem with this method is that I'm guessing it will automatically attempt to load the package from the standard Anaconda channel before loading it from the proper channel. Anyways I'll leave this to people who know what they're doing.

@joelhullcio, even with the recursion bug fixed, you're not going to be able to build a local repository in the manner you're seeking. This is because you need the updated package metadata that exists on repo.continuum.io; the data contained in the `.tar.bz2` files themselves is out of date. We've been making adjustments to the metadata like this for some time, and it certainly is nice not having to rebuild packages just to make changes to the metadata; but this is an instance where it causes a failure.

What I think we would need to do is instead of doing `conda index`, we build a short script that pulls down the `repodata.json` file from continuum.io, filter out those packages that are present in the local cache, and write out that filtered version of `repodata.json`. Arguably, this capability could and should be built into `conda index`.

this happens because you call once `.set("init_key", "init_value")` outside the for-loop, and is what you are receiving in the output.

every call you do on `test_update_query` creates a new object -- it means, it does not change the object assigned to that variable.
therefore, in your for-loop, you need to assign the new query object to your existing variable, if you want to receive the returned value:

```
import pypika
from datetime import datetime

if __name__ == '__main__':
	table_to_update = pypika.Table("test_table")
	columns_test = {"key1" : "value1", "key2" : "value2"}
	test_update_query = pypika.Query.update(table_to_update).where(table_to_update.id == 1).set("init_key", "init_value")
	for key in columns_test:
		test_update_query = test_update_query.set(key, columns_test[key])  # note the assignment to the variable here
	print(str(test_update_query))
```
Then you should receive: 
`UPDATE "test_table" SET "init_key"='init_value',"key1"='value1',"key2"='value2' WHERE "id"=1`

does that work for you?

Here is another approach to this problem: since the number of output units is small (21 in the default setting), we can implement this directly in Theano, "manually" slicing the input for each output neuron. I have implemented this and it doesn't seem to add too much computational overhead:

```
import theano.tensor as T
import theano

def pooling_fixed_output_size(inputs, output_size, pooling_op=T.max):
    """ Performs a pooling operation that results in a fixed size. Refer
        to appendix A in [1]

        Parameters
        ----------
        inputs : tensor with 4 dimensions (N x C x H x W)
        output_size: integer
        pooling_op: the pooling operation (default: MAX)

        Returns a tensor with 3 dimensions (N x C x output_size*output_size)
        
        [1] He, Kaiming et al (2015):
           Spatial Pyramid Pooling in Deep Convolutional Networks
           for Visual Recognition.
           http://arxiv.org/pdf/1406.4729.pdf.
    """
    h, w = inputs.shape[2:]

    result = []
    n = float(output_size)
    for row in range(output_size):
        for col in range(output_size):
            start_h = T.floor((row)/n*h).astype('int32')
            end_h = T.ceil((row+1)/n*h).astype('int32')
            start_w = T.floor((col)/n*w).astype('int32')
            end_w = T.ceil((col+1)/n*w).astype('int32')

            this_result = pooling_op(inputs[:,:,start_h:end_h,start_w:end_w], axis=(2,3))
            result.append(this_result.dimshuffle(0,1,'x'))
    return  T.concatenate(result, axis=2)

def SpatialPyramidPooling(inputs, pool_sizes=[4,2,1]):
    """ Spatial Pyramid Pooling 
    
    """
    result = []
    for p in pool_sizes:
        result.append(pooling_fixed_output_size(inputs, p))
    return T.concatenate(result, axis=2)
```

Note that this implementation gives the same result as the current implementation for some input sizes, but not all, so they are not interchangeable. For example, for an input size of 10, using this new implementation (as described in the appendix of paper), we already have non-uniform strides:.
```

xxxxxxxxxx
www
  www
     www
       www
```

Nevertheless, here is the code to check that this new implementation gives the same answer for a 20x20 input:

```
# Compile function to get results of this new implementation
inputs = T.tensor4()
output = SpatialPyramidPooling(inputs)
get_output_new_spp = theano.function([inputs], output)


# Compile function to get results of the current dnn.SPP implementation
import lasagne
from lasagne.layers.dnn import SpatialPyramidPoolingDNNLayer
from lasagne.layers import InputLayer

i = InputLayer((None,None,None,None))
spp_layer = SpatialPyramidPoolingDNNLayer(i)

output_dnn_spp = lasagne.layers.get_output(spp_layer)
get_output_dnn_spp = theano.function([i.input_var], output_dnn_spp)


# Check if both are equal
import numpy as np
feature_maps = np.random.random((100, 128, 20,20)).astype(np.float32)
np.allclose(get_output_new_spp(feature_maps), get_output_dnn_spp(feature_maps))
# returns True
```


I tested this on an NVIDIA P-100 GPU, and this method was between 50% and 100% slower than the current implementation, depending on the input sizes. However, this cost is often a small fraction of the forward propagation time.

```
feature_maps = np.random.random((100, 128, 4,4)).astype(np.float32)
%timeit get_output_new_spp(feature_maps)
#100 loops, best of 3: 3.31 ms per loop
%timeit get_output_dnn_spp(feature_maps)
#1000 loops, best of 3: 1.6 ms per loop

feature_maps = np.random.random((100, 128, 20,20)).astype(np.float32)
%timeit get_output_new_spp(feature_maps)
#100 loops, best of 3: 5.61 ms per loop
%timeit get_output_dnn_spp(feature_maps)
#100 loops, best of 3: 4.09 ms per loop
```

Turns out I do! It's the one used for this comment: https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/pull/394#issuecomment-134597468.
```python
import sys
import numpy as np
import theano
import theano.tensor as T
floatX = theano.config.floatX
from theano.sandbox.cuda import as_cuda_ndarray_variable
from theano.sandbox.cuda import synchronize as sync
import lasagne

batch_size = 64
channels = 3
image_size = (50, 50)
input_shape = (batch_size, channels)
factor = 1.5
if sys.argv[1] == 'fixed':
	input_shape = input_shape + image_size
elif sys.argv[1] == 'dynamic':
	input_shape = input_shape + (None, None)
else:
	raise NotImplementedError("First argument must be 'fixed' or 'dynamic'")
input_layer = lasagne.layers.InputLayer(input_shape)
loc_layer = lasagne.layers.InputLayer((batch_size, 6))
trans = lasagne.layers.TransformerLayer(input_layer, loc_layer,
		downsample_factor=factor)

x = input_layer.input_var
l = loc_layer.input_var
y = lasagne.layers.get_output(trans)

X = np.random.randn(batch_size, channels, *image_size).astype(floatX)
L = np.random.rand(batch_size, 6).astype(floatX)
fn_fwd = theano.function([], as_cuda_ndarray_variable(y), givens={x: X, l: L})

gy = T.tensor4()
Y = np.random.randn(batch_size, channels,
		int(image_size[0]/factor), int(image_size[1]/factor)).astype(floatX)
gx, gl = theano.grad(None, wrt=[x, l], known_grads={y: gy})
gx = as_cuda_ndarray_variable(gx)
gl = as_cuda_ndarray_variable(gl)
fn_bwd = theano.function([], [gx, gl], givens={x: X, l: L, gy: Y},
		on_unused_input='ignore')

if sys.argv[2] == 'fwd':
	fn = fn_fwd
elif sys.argv[2] == 'bwd':
	fn = fn_bwd
else:
	raise NotImplementedError("Second argument must be 'fwd' or 'bwd'")

import timeit
print min(timeit.repeat('fn(); fn(); fn(); fn(); fn(); sync()', 'from __main__ import fn, sync', repeat=50, number=1))
```
The first command line should not make a difference (fixed or dynamic), the second one changes between forward and backward pass (fwd and bwd).

> What about: 4. Make `Layer.params` a list, store tags as a list on each `param`.

(A `set` would be better for the latter, we don't need them to be ordered.)

> I think the main objection was that having params shared across layers might cause tags to be overwritten, but I think we an equivalent situation currently: if different layers have contradictory tags for a shared param, it will give confusing results when trying to filter `get_all_params`.

The objection is also about parameters shared across different networks (e.g., when taking a parameter of an existing network and using it to create a layer in a different network). Another objection was that we explicitly want to tag our parameters, not the Theano graph (unlike `blocks`), so it would be good to have the tagging encapsulated in the `Layer` class and not touch the expressions constituting the graph. However, an advantage of tying the tags to the shared parameters would be that you could still easily obtain the tags of each parameter after collecting them via `get_all_params`.

In any case, this discussion probably belongs into #164 instead of here, as we need a different `OrderedDict` implementation regardless of what we do with `Layer.params`.

I'll gladly add more options to try and identify the biomass reaction, although changing the ID for the reaction is a super simple fix for model builders. In that sense I'd rather attempt to unify what this particular reaction is called by leaving the heuristic strict. But I'm waiting to hear more opinions on this.

I'm not convinced of the other suggestions:
1. Some lumped reactions might hypothetically consume +5 substrate, consume ATP and be the objective function (in a production strain for instance), hence basing the test on these criteria might not be robust enough.
2. Any cell be it by itself or in a community/ organ should need to construct structural macromolecules to function and by extension a biomass reaction would have to be present. The only exception that comes to my mind here are erythrocytes. But frankly, I cannot asses how common models without a biomass reaction would be. 

So, my suggestion is to detect it by preferably one very robust criterion. I'm happy to rely on the community to discuss and agree on whatever that may be. If this identification for a biomass reaction cannot be identified in a model, all tests pertaining to the Biomass reaction and precursor production are skipped.

Hello.  You're using `name_only=True` in the `refreshall()` method.  This pulls only the names of the VirtualRouters from the firewall, not their properties.  So you won't get any of the interface information.  Remove the `name_only=True` and it should work fine.

FYI, `name_only=True` is most useful when you're trying to pull objects that have very large XML, but all you really need is a list of the objects to operate on.  A primary use case for this is when you need to know what vsys exist on a firewall, but you don't want to pull all the massive configuration within these vsys just to know that the vsys exist.  Let me know if any questions on this.

In that naive example we have overfit though - With those weights, the training model has become as closely fit as it will ever be. Let's say you have 100 samples and you weight the first 50 samples at 2 and the next 50 at 1 - if it learns the first 50 perfectly and doesn't learn the next 50, you'll end up with a WER of 0.66 with the weighted training set, or 0.5 if you unweighted it. Contrary to what you suggest, if you calculated the WER on the unweighted training set, you're likely to under-report WER and end up not catching overfitting. In your example, we would never over-fit.

I think this behavior is due to how CloudFormation works:

* Senza `create` creates a new CloudFormation (CF) stack with VersionDomain and MainDomain DNS records
  * MainDomain has weight zero for this stack version and is a "managed by CF" resource
* Senza `traffic` will change the weight of the MainDomain record without changing the CF template
  * MainDomain is therefore no longer exactly the same resource as originally created by CF
* Senza `delete` will delete the CF stack
  * MainDomain will not be deleted by CF as the resource no longer matches the original "CF managed" resource

So basically we are mixing CloudFormation and manual changes (directly using Route 53 API).

@Fruchuxs - As greg mentions, the fact that it is looking for source code means that you found the symbol file (PDB).   You will actually see which particular one if you look at the Debug -> Modules display for that DLL.   

The fact that it is looking on your machine (in the original location it was built (on some other machine). means it is trying to find the source.    This means for whatever reason it could not find it with SourceLink.    This is what Greg is trying to debug with his information.  

Now that could be because you are running a version of the runtime that does not have source link information (that is the git URL) in its PDBs, or that you are running a version of Visual Studio that does not support Source Link (you need VS 2017), or something about the configuration is wrong.   

Note that you can work around this problem by fetching the source you care about from GIT and pointing VS at it.   If you right click on your *.sln file in the solution explorer and select Properties -> Common Properties -> Debug Source Files, you will see all the places that it tries to look, as well as the files it had prompted you for and failed (it will not prompt again).  

What I do is clear the set of failed files and then try to step through again.  It will prompt you and now you have a file to give it.  Once you give in one, it will figure out to look near it for other files so you tend to not have to give it much help (but you can by putting directories in the 'Debug Source Files' dialog.

That's not the exact term, but I used that to describe a mecanism used by tar archives:

basically, tar archives are a collection of files, refered to by their absolute path within the archive. For instance, if the archive has two members
```
/foo.txt
/bar/baz.txt
```
then you have to infer that `bar` is a directory within the archive.

But since you may want to attribute special permissions or whatever to a directory, it is also possible to add a directory to the tar archive by declaring it to be of `DIRTYPE`: for instance the same archive could come as 
```
/foo.txt
/bar/
/bar/baz.txt
```
and still have the same structure.

So I refer to the first case as an *inferred* directory, and to the second as a *declared* directory (but that's just me trying to find a good description for both cases when I fill comments within the source).

While utf8mb4 is required for MySQL/MariaDB servers to store the full UTF-8 character sets I don't see how this is useful for constance. Using Emoji or other characters covered by utf8mb4 are hardly useful for configuration variable names. In other words, this is an extreme edge case and a required data migration to support it is way out of scope of constance.

If you still want to use utf8mb4 in your app for other models, feel free to use it when instantiating the database and then change the collation of the `constance_config` table back to the default (utf8_generic_ci or similar, but check your database documentation for which it is).

Making the database model pluggable is **not** an alternative given the increased implementation and operational complexity.

It takes some words to clarify. 

Currently, Ray has a plasma manager (`src/plasma/plasma_manager.cc`) for itself and shares some codes and formats with Arrow and `src/plasma/format/plasma.fbs` is one of them. 

However, Ray does not implement a plasma client for itself. Instead, it uses Arrow's client using Arrow's `plasma.fbs`.

And now, Ray's plasma manager uses an event loop (so it's async) but Arrow's plasma client doesn't, so I have to override parts of codes of Arrow's plasma client. To override it, Arrow's `plasma.fbs` is copied from Arrow's repo. 

Arrow's `plasma.fbs` is almost identical to Ray's but there's still a little difference between them. If we want to keep only one copy of `plasma.fbs`, a better way may be abandoning Arrow's plasma client (by copying Arrow plasma client's code back to Ray and add event loops). However, this will permanently change Ray's behavior, making Ray totally async. A workaround may be keeping two different APIs for plasma's client that one is async and another is blocking.

My problem with this PR is the Members and the Members_s classes of the GTM pools code. It seems to me to break all of the existing conventions we have for Resources, Collections, and OrganizingCollections. I don't think it should be in the business of being a big conditional statement. I think those resources should be individual classes.

It's current design makes me think "premature optimization", whether that was its intention or not. That design though, limits our flexibility with modifying these resources in the future, and makes future maintenance difficult. If any refactoring needs to take place or overriding functionality (consider _update or _create, etc) needs to happen for a specific endpoint to address breaking functionality in the underlying API, this design limits us.

I also think the introduction of a new meta_data variable (v12_gtm_pool_type) is a bad idea. It's used in this case solely for the purposes of addressing a _deficiency_ in the classes design. That suggests bad design and therefore a need to correct it.

Meta data really should be concerned with more general purpose stuff and less class specific stuff. What happens if it changes in v12.1 or v13. Do we add more metadata to cover those cases? I think appropriately named classes can easily be created out of these conditional statements, and then to handle their "different" class names (to provide a consistent user experience) one can simply override the class name as shown here

https://github.com/F5Networks/f5-common-python/pull/664/files#diff-d78a02f174da6910dd417302d09c5aa9R78

So I would look for those changes (unless I'm convinced otherwise) before I would +1 the PR.

Hi @leycec, thanks for this request! I was not aware yet that scipy was deprecating their imageio functionality and putting imageio forward as a replacement. I agree that we should try to make the API more conformant to that of scipy to ease the transition. We might also setup a small doc page to help people in their transition.

The way that imageio's `imread()` works is that it accepts `**kwargs`, which are passed to the used format. What args are accepted is documented per format, e.g. here for [Pillow PNG](http://imageio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/format_png-pil.html). If a format behaves well, it will raise an error when it receives keyword arguments that it does not know. 

The Pillow plugin provides many formats, so it looks like we may want to add a common option (i.e. keyword argument) to all formats that the Pillow plugin provides. Further down the line, we can see if we can add the same keyword argument to other formats as well. Perhaps not for mode, since that seems specific to Pillow, but I can see it makes sense for `as_gray`.

I think that the following code fix previous issues:

```
def strahler_order(section):
    '''Branching order of a tree section

    The strahler order is the inverse of the branch order,
    since this is computed from the tips of the tree
    towards the root.
    
    This implementation is a translation of the three steps described in
    Wikipedia (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strahler_number):
     - If the node is a leaf (has no children), its Strahler number is one.
     - If the node has one child with Strahler number i, and all other children
       have Strahler numbers less than i, then the Strahler number of the node 
       is i again.
     - If the node has two or more children with Strahler number i, and no 
       children with greater number, then the Strahler number of the node is 
       i + 1.

    No efforts have been invested in making it computationnaly efficient, but
    it computes acceptably fast on tested morphologies (i.e., no waiting time).
    '''

    if len(section.children) == 0:
        return 1
    else:
        child_orders = np.array([strahler_order(child) for child in section.children])
        max_so_children = max(child_orders)
        nb_max          = np.sum(child_orders == max_so_children)
        if nb_max == 1:
            return max_so_children
        else:
            return max_so_children+1
```

This is currently both the intended behavior **and** an annoying bug (or at least a confusing behavior).

The way saving works is by:
- components (returned by `get_components`) are handled by the dynet loading/saving mechanism.
- **everything else** (things in the object's dict) is handled by python's pickling mechanism (components are specifically removed from the pickling in `__getstate__` of saveable because they are not saveable)

I guess what we need is a mechanism for things that are part of the object, are not parameters/components, but should not be pickled. One way to add this is an `ignore_fields` method in `Saveable`, and then remove these things in `__getstate__`. (and maybe there is more "standard" name for this method in the pickle protocol?)
If you add this and do some minimal testing (and document it?) I will happily approve the PR
(or I can get to it at some point in the future).

One question though, is how were you planning to later on load this saved object? Support for this could be nice also.

Yeah maybe, the failing tests (which I haven't digested yet) might suggest this as well. Here's a slightly more fleshed out use case to describe where I'm coming from:

In practice the Upload and UploadFromManifest tasks (which I actually do use), have several more parameters:

```
class RequestMixin(object):
    url = luigi.Parameter()
    headers = DictParameter(default={})
    params = DictParameter(default={})
    username = luigi.Parameter(default=None, significant=False)
    password = luigi.Parameter(default=None, significant=False)

class Upload(luigi.Task, RequestMixin):
    payload_file = luigi.Parameter()

    def run(self):
        # builds and sends request, saves response content as task output

class UploadFromManifest(luigi.Task, RequestMixin):
    manifest = luigi.Parameter()
    def run(self):
         yield [self.clone(cls=Upload, payload_file=file) for file in open(self.manifest).readlines()]
```

we use these the generic UploadFromManifest in several different pipelines to transfer data between services. In most concrete cases all of the parameters in in RequestMixin are either known at task-definition time, or derived from a more context-specific parameter. So they might be specifiable as something like:

```
class UploadToServiceFoo(UploadFromManifest):
    release_number = luigi.IntParameter()

    # static for this task
    username = settings.FooServiceUser
    password = settings.FooServicePassword
    headers = {'Content-Type': 'text/csv'}
    params = {'uploader: 'luigi'}
  
    #url derives from release_number
    @property
    def url(self):
        return "http://foo.com/%s" % self.release_number
```

This PR makes the above code functional, and I rather like its flat+declarative structure. Without it you'd need to write:

```
class UploadToService(luigi.WrapperTask):
    <same declarations as above>

    def requires(self):
         yield UploadManifest(username=self.username, password=self.password, headers=self.headers, params=self.params, url=self.url)
```

ie you lose the intended benefits of clone (not having to re-enumerate every parameter when describing parameters). Furthermore the first example throws an exception like `missing required parameter url`, which surprised me since url is clearly defined by the task.

Another small consideration:  the following code works currently (maybe you'd consider it an antipattern, I'm not sure):

```
class SpecificUpload(Upload):
    username = settings.username
    password = settings.password
    headers = {'Content-Type':'text/csv'}    
    url = 'http://foo.com'
```

In other words, overriding parameters with class-level attributes or properties currently works, but propagating these overrides through clone does not. That feels a little asymmetrical to me. 

However, maybe you disagree, or there's another mechanism for addressing this use case that I'm missing?

Small update on this; with my new segmented downloader code I have managed to get Streamlink in-sync with Twitch for non-low latency videos, using `--hls-live-edge 1`. I am still working on low latency streams. 

However, Streamlink will likely never be able to reduce the delay to the same as Twitch. This is because the Twitch player _speeds up_ the video if it is lagging behind and it has sufficient future video buffered. Using Streamlink, if you experience any buffering that's it, you will lag behind until you restart the stream. I do not think this is avoidable as it is the job of the player to control the playback speed.

Ah, right, I remember now. Just the wages of using internals for workarounds I'm afraid, part of the pains of bearing with us while we grow the code base :)

In general, the published API is warranted to be stable; the few occasional changes to that we can't avoid get documented as backward incompatibilities.

The internals on the other hand, are subject to change on any given commit. They don't usually of course, but from time to time they do as we reorganize the code base or whatever. Interim workarounds to implement features are very useful and I definitely endorse them as a way to complete apps while we're still building, but unfortunately vulnerable to reorganizations like this and have to be fixed up from time to time when updating to a new version.

Hi @jashsayani ,

Our documentation says

> If you’re sure that this command executes successfully without human intervention, you can add the command to crontab (since certificates are only renewed when they’re determined to be near expiry, the command can run on a regular basis, like every week or every day). In that case, you are likely to want to use the -q or --quiet quiet flag to silence all output except errors.

https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html?highlight=crontab

Elsewhere we've suggested running twice per day. Some ways of installing Certbot take care of this for you automatically.

The default behavior of `certbot renew` is to renew certificates that are expiring in less than 30 days. So, the scenario you were concerned with won't occur. Your certificates will ordinarily be renewed long before they are due to expire. (Certificates are allowed to have overlapping validity, so the previous certificate and new certificate will both continue to be valid for a period of about 30 days.)

> can a deamon (say certbotd) run in the background and take care of renewal upon expiration without setting up a cronjob?

That would be fine, but we've never implemented anything like this. Since most systems have `cron` and/or `systemd` for periodic task scheduling, creating our own daemon feels like extra work and memory use for no obvious benefit. (It might appear to be a benefit that it could perform the renewal at *the exact right moment*, but we think `cron` is probably actually quite appropriate for this because the renewal attempt can fail for random and often transitory reasons, in which case it should be re-attempted periodically.)

Again, the extra load on size during client-handshake is not worth the extra risk with a smaller keysize. The ones recommending against using 4096 bits RSA fail to come up with valid arguments not to. Not to mention the fact that we're relying on [proper generation of the keys](https://crocs.fi.muni.cz/public/papers/rsa_ccs17), and if ever that ends up being flawed, we're way better off with 4096 bits keys.
Check this; https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/489#issuecomment-114083162
You want to make sure you generate Diffie Helman keys of >=4096 bits as well, stuff like this
`openssl dhparam -out /etc/nginx/ssl/dhparam.pem 4096`
if you want it to make any sense when you've enabled DH(E) in your cypher suites.

1. Good question. [Platform-specific deps can be expressed with setuptools](https://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/setuptools.html#declaring-platform-specific-dependencies), which is nice. If this corner case happens, we may have to expand the matrix to {Python 2 versions} * {oldest-supported Python 2 deps, newest deps} + {Python 3 versions} * {oldest-supported Python 3 deps, newest deps}. Do you see this scenario coming? I'd try other things first, like nudging the distro to package a newer version of the dep.
2. A dep shouldn't drop support for a Python version without itself having a major version bump, so our pinnings should take care of us. Otherwise, we'll have to retroactively pin the upper end of our range more tightly.
3. Instead, I would add a recommendation to "pin back" major versions in our documentation for plugin developers. It's extra work, and it's unlikely that whatever combination of packages we pin would be representative of what end-users would see anyway. Letting devs see breakage when pip-installing their stuff would incent them to fix it.

Sure, why not? You check out what you want, then you do `pip install -e .` in the checkout dirs.

I think we could reliably update the server. While very much pseudocode, we could do something like this:

```
import acme

if config.server == "https://acme-v01.api.letsencrypt.org/directory" and acme.__version__ >= "1.2.3":
    config.server = "https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory"
```

One of the problems we'll have here is maybe people manually set the server value to the v01 endpoint and don't want it changed (although that'd likely break their client).

Additionally, if we do something like this, it may cause similar breakage to what this PR is trying to fix later down the road. If people use their renewal config files with an old version of the client, it will incorrectly try to use the v02 endpoint and fail.

`OPENSSL_sk_num` is a symbol used under the covers for manipulating stack structures. It was added in 1.1.0 and did not exist prior to that, so the problem here is likely that it's looking at the 1.1.0 include headers during compile but then loading an older OpenSSL.

I really cannot emphasize enough that it is extremely difficult to install your own custom OpenSSL and expect it to work reliably. The way the compiler does include and linker paths can be extremely counterintuitive.

As an update, a colleague gave me an estimate which indicated that over 10% of TLS key exchanges in a particular context are non-PFS and hence would benefit from the eventual destruction of their server private key material. We can look for other views of this which might show higher or lower numbers, but that was based on a pretty big sample so it should be relatively typical of what data sources might show. (Of course it's different from country to country, and from site to site depending on the fraction of mobile users, etc.)

SSH is a service that should be listed in the hostSystem.configManager.serviceSystem.service
```
serviceSystem = hostSystem.configManager.serviceSystem
sshService = [x for x in serviceSystem.serviceInfo.service if x.key == 'TSM-SSH'][0]
if not sshService.running:
   serviceSystem.Start(sshService.key)
...
serviceSystem.Stop(sshService.key) # Done with it, can disable now.
```
Do note that there is a default timeout for the SSH service to disable itself.
If you want to change that value you can use the advancedOption and update it.
```
from pyVmomi import VmomiSupport
optManager = hostSystem.configManager.advancedOption
sshTimeout = optManager.QueryView('UserVars.ESXiShellTimeOut')
print("SSH will disable itself after %d seconds" % sshTimeout[0].value)
# Now update it to 1 minute.
sshTimeout[0].value = VmomiSupport.vmodlTypes['long'](60)
optManager.UpdateValues(sshTimeout)
```

It uses the target as the metric name. It wouldn't chomp off the subdomain - `www` - or the tld - `org` - as that would have the possibility of causing overlapping metric collection.

As an example, the following would collide:

```
admin.example.com
example.org
```

Those are obviously two different domains, but by your logic, we'd have them both report to `example`, which is definitely wrong. Instead, we simply replace periods in the key to create the metric name.

Hope that helps.

Alright, thanks for elaborating

There are different ways to approach the fine tuning, which depends on whether there are changes to only the weights parameters or also to the model architecture (symbol). General idea is to pick whatever you like from the pre-trained model and weights and decide which weights should be fixed. Note that by default, all weights are retrained.

If what you want is to keep the same VGG  model structure but only modify the last one for which the number of hidden units is the number of categories (so two 4096 hidden unist FC layers followed by a FC layer which hidden units match your number of categories), then you would probably what to start the fine-tuning following the last dropout, something like: 
`drop7 <- internals$get.output(which(outputs == "fc7_output"))`

Then, you can continue the composition of the symbol like for any model designed from scratch. 
Here the only two remaining elements as can be seen from the [Model Zoo](http://data.dmlc.ml/models/imagenet/vgg/vgg16-symbol.json) are the last FC and the SoftMaxOutput: 

```
fc_final <- mx.symbol.FullyConnected(data = drop7, num.hidden = you number of levels)
softmax <- mx.symbol.SoftmaxOutput(data = fc_final)
```

You can now train using `mx.model.FeedForward.create` and feeding it with `softmax` as a symbol. 
`mx.model.FeedForward.create` has parameters to specify initial weights of the model (arg.params) and whether some parameters must be kept fixed (fixed.param). If you want to have all parameters prior to fc6 to remain fixed, then you will need to pass the vector of these parameters into the `fixed.param` argument to that their weights are ignored during update. 
To reuse the VGG-16 weights, you will also need to feed the `arg.param` argument. This will be the list of weights obtained from the pre-trained model, except for the final FC layer where you would apply a random initialization as illustrated in the cat-dog example.

Hi @lu4,

You can create a clone of your network, and then make adjustments during the copy. If you're using a Sequential Block as a container for your network, you could create another Sequential Block and add all of the layers from one network to the other, which would save redefining the network. You would make changes to the necessary layers before adding them to the new Sequential Block.

As I understand the problem, you'll need to change the weights and biases for the layer you want to expand, and the weights for the next dense layer (as the weights shape depends on the units in the layer before which has changed). After constructing the news weights and biases (i.e. padding with 0s), you can then use [`set_data`](https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/api/python/gluon/gluon.html?highlight=set_data#mxnet.gluon.Parameter.set_data) on the parameters of interest before adding to the new Sequential Block.

Unfortunately I don't think you can't mutate the original network like this, because you're changing the shape of the parameters. You'll hit shape assertion errors. And you can't just swap out a single layer in the original Sequential Block because they don't support assignment.

@piiswrong This is largely syntactic sugar (imagine tuning `kernel_size`, `dilation`, and `stride`; the user can simply set `padding=-1` once to keep layer sizes constant). If we support modes like `'full'` (or `'valid'`, which is equivalent to `padding=0`) in the future, the API could look like:

`padding: int, str, or list of int/strs, where str is one of 'same', 'full', etc.`

and one might write:

`nn.Conv3D(channels=300, kernel_size=(3, 5, 7), padding=('same', 0, 'full'))`

However, there is **novelty** when `stride > 1`; the input size needs to be known, as padding no longer simplifies to `dilated_kernel_size // 2`. I just saw that Theano compromised here; it defines `'half'` as this quantity outright, and never implemented `'same'`: http://deeplearning.net/software/theano/library/tensor/nnet/conv.html

@szha I think this novelty makes it hard to do on the front-end, but I'd love to be corrected. A practical approach could be to ship, in order:
1. A frontend version (`stride=1`, symmetrical), with API
2. A backend version (arb. stride, symmetrical)
3. An asymmetrical backend version

The first is a quick win people would use (e.g., porting models from other frameworks). We can implement the rest as demand permits. I'm seeing more how hard things like (3) are, as e.g., cuDNN's API doesn't support it.

@johnarnold sorry to get back on this so late. 

Just realized that in the case where there is no impersonation, all the environment variables are _already_ being passed down to the task execution process, which means they are already accessible to all operators. This PR simply extends this behavior when user impersonation is specified. 

That said, I still believe we have a potential security concern on the whole by passing down all environment variables to the local task execution. This means that it is the responsibility of (1) the airflow admin to not store sensitive data in environment variables in production or (2) the dag maintainer to properly audit the dag file and make sure it is not malicious. (1) seems very hard to guarantee (2) seems easier, but not foolproof.

I'm okay with merging this PR since it's just extending existing behavior. Long term I think we should address this issue, so I'll create a JIRA for this.
